Lemongrass grilled chicken with rice noodles

english: Grilled Chicken Rice Noodles with Lemongrass
Lemongrass grilled chicken with rice noodles

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for grilled lemongrass chicken vermicelli. FOODON_00002473 (chicken dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (grilled chicken meat) used as closest parent; no specific lemongrass chicken vermicelli composite code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for grilled lemongrass chicken vermicelli composite dish.

main_ingredients: fresh herbs (mint, Vietnamese coriander, bean sprouts)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising a bowl of rice vermicelli noodles topped with 120–150 g of grilled lemongrass-marinated chicken, fresh herbs, bean sprouts, and nuoc cham dipping sauce on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Bún Gà Nướng (Vietnamese grilled chicken vermicelli) — composite dish estimate based on USDA FoodData Central entries for grilled chicken (NDB 05064), rice vermicelli (NDB 20050), and fresh herb garnish components; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Vietnamese dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central / FAO INFOODS SEA
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for grilled lemongrass chicken vermicelli exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values estimated from USDA FoodData Central grilled chicken breast (FDC ID 171477), cooked rice vermicelli (FDC ID 168882), and typical Vietnamese herb garnish proportions. Dish composition assumed: ~35% grilled chicken, ~45% cooked rice vermicelli, ~10% fresh herbs/vegetables, ~10% nuoc cham sauce. Per-100g values are weighted composite estimates.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli, boiled — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; composite dish GI estimated by weighted carbohydrate contribution method
gi_source.source_match_notes: Rice vermicelli (bún) has a published GI of approximately 58 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Grilled chicken contributes negligible glycemic load. Fresh herbs and nuoc cham contribute minimal carbohydrate. Composite dish GI estimated at 53 (low) after weighting by carbohydrate contribution: rice vermicelli dominates glycemic response but is diluted by protein-rich chicken and low-carbohydrate garnish components. GL per serving = (GI/100) × carbohydrate_g_per_serving = (53/100) × 59.2 g carbohydrate (400 g × 14.8 g/100g) = 31.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce and nuoc cham
hypertension.guidance: Each 400 g serving provides approximately 1240 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced fish sauce or nuoc cham on the side and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low GI of 53 but a glycemic load of approximately 31 per standard serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion of noodles, increase the proportion of grilled chicken and fresh vegetables, and monitor postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat and lean grilled protein
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides lean grilled chicken with low saturated fat (0.5 g per 100 g) and no added oils in the grilling process, making it a relatively heart-friendly option; however, sodium from fish sauce should be monitored in patients with concurrent hypertension.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Lemongrass Chicken Vermicelli (Vietnamese: Bún Gà Nướng Sả) is a Vietnamese-origin dish popular in Singapore's food delivery market. It consists of tender chicken pieces — typically thigh or breast — marinated in a fragrant blend of lemongrass, garlic, shallots, fish sauce, and sugar, then grilled over high heat to achieve a caramelised, slightly charred exterior with a juicy interior. The chicken is served over a bed of thin rice vermicelli noodles (bún), accompanied by fresh herbs such as Vietnamese coriander (rau răm), mint, shredded lettuce, and bean sprouts, with a side of nuoc cham dipping sauce made from fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, garlic, and chilli. The dish delivers a balance of savoury, sweet, and citrusy flavours with contrasting textures — tender grilled meat, soft noodles, and crisp fresh vegetables. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dish (~118 kcal per 100 g) with a good protein content (~10 g per 100 g) from lean grilled chicken, moderate carbohydrate from rice vermicelli, and low saturated fat. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low), though the glycemic load per standard 400 g serving is approximately 31, which is clinically relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is moderate-to-high due to fish sauce and nuoc cham, requiring attention for hypertensive patients. The dish is dairy-free and generally halal-suitable when prepared with halal-certified chicken and fish sauce.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for grilled lemongrass chicken vermicelli exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional food composition database. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for grilled chicken breast and cooked rice vermicelli, weighted by estimated dish composition. GI is estimated from published rice vermicelli GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database, adjusted for composite dish dilution. Confidence is medium due to reliance on component-level estimation rather than a direct dish analysis.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries used for this composite estimate. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data for this dish composition. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source data for this composite estimate. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0 as grilled chicken and rice vermicelli contain no industrially produced trans fats; confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central source data. GI estimated at 53 (low) based on rice vermicelli GI of ~58 from University of Sydney GI Database, adjusted downward for composite dish dilution by protein-rich chicken and low-carbohydrate garnish. GL per serving calculated as (53/100) × 59.2 g = 31.4. Dish served at Viet'Spice - Hương vị Việt, Circular Road, Singapore (field survey). Price SGD 18.90. This dish is Vietnamese in origin but served in Singapore context; multilingual names provided for English, Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and Vietnamese per Singaporean cuisine multilingual rules, with Vietnamese also included given the dish's Vietnamese origin.
